title: Host never relays PLAYER_UPDATE → remote players frozen at spawn in 3+ player sessions
detail: Topology is a star: guests connect only to the host (SignalingService.connectToHost, line 117). On receiving a guest's position the host only updates its own store and stops: `case 'PLAYER_UPDATE': store.updateRemotePlayer(message.payload.id, message.payload); break;` — no rebroadcast to other peers. The periodic STATE_SYNC payload (lines 448-458) carries `machines/gameTime/weather/emergencyActive` but NO player positions. So guest A's movement reaches the host and dies there; guest B never receives it and renders A frozen at the spawn position `[0, 1.7, 0]` set in addRemotePlayer (line 178) / PLAYER_JOIN handler (line 292). Two-player sessions work (host-guest is direct), which masks the bug.
fix: In the host branch of the PLAYER_UPDATE case, after updating the store, rebroadcast the update to all other peers excluding the sender: `if (store.isHost) this.broadcast(message, peerId);`. Verify against the message contract before shipping.

title: Join-timeout destroys singleton without nulling it → subsequent multiplayer attempts use a poisoned manager
detail: joinRoom's connection-timeout callback calls `this.destroy()` (line 103) which sets `isDestroyed = true` but does NOT null the module-level `multiplayerManager` singleton — only the exported `destroyMultiplayerManager()` (line 680) does. After a join times out, the next `getMultiplayerManager()` (line 673) returns the same poisoned instance, and every broadcast/state-sync interval body short-circuits via `if (this.isDestroyed) return` (lines 419, 443, 464). Normal leave is fine because UI calls leave() then destroyMultiplayerManager() (MultiplayerLobby.tsx:99-100, MultiplayerPanel.tsx:130-131); the internal destroy() paths (joinRoom timeout, and any future internal teardown) are the gap.
fix: Have internal teardown null the singleton: call the module-level destroyMultiplayerManager() from the joinRoom timeout instead of this.destroy(), or add an isDestroyed reset/recreate guard in getMultiplayerManager() so a destroyed instance is replaced. Confirm no double-destroy regression first.

title: Stale-peer sweep drops a still-open DataConnection without closing it (leak on disconnect)
detail: The ping interval detects stale peers and routes them to handlePeerDisconnected (lines 486-487). handlePeerDisconnected only does `this.peerConnections.delete(peerId)` (line 274) and never calls `peerConn.close()`. On the normal 'close' event path the underlying DataConnection is already closing, but on the STALE path the connection is still open (stale = no messages for 5s, not closed). The PeerConnection wrapper and its underlying WebRTC DataConnection are dropped from the map while still open, leaking the connection and its handlers.
fix: In handlePeerDisconnected (or before deletion in the stale sweep), close the wrapper: `this.peerConnections.get(peerId)?.close();` prior to `this.peerConnections.delete(peerId);`. PeerConnection.close() already guards isOpen and calls connection.close().

title: VCP encoder/decoder roundtrip collision: 'mastery' encodes to 'M' and decodes to 'meaning'
detail: FlourishingDimension includes both 'meaning' and 'mastery' (types.ts:130-136). The encoder writes the concern dimension as `concernDimension[0].toUpperCase()` (encoder.ts:114-115), so both 'meaning' and 'mastery' encode to the single letter 'M'. The wellbeing decode regex `/\[WELL:F(\d+)([↑→↓])\|?([MMCJWA])?\]/u` (decoder.ts:36 — note the duplicated 'M' in the char class) plus dimMap `{ M:'meaning', A:'agency', C:'connection', J:'joy', W:'wholeness' }` (decoder.ts:154-160) has NO entry for 'mastery'. Result: encoding concernDimension='mastery' decodes back to 'meaning' (silent data corruption). Same defect in the reasoning layer: encoder.ts:211 `flourishingFocus[0].toUpperCase()` vs decoder.ts:324-330 dimMap, also missing 'mastery'. Existing test uses concernDimension:'agency' (encoding.test.ts:74) so the collision is untested/latent. Decode is currently only re-exported (vcp/index.ts) with no found UI/state consumer, so impact is on cross-system interop/debugging, not live UI.
fix: Pick a distinct wire symbol for 'mastery' (e.g. 'Y' or lowercase 'm') and update all three places in lockstep: encoder mapping, the WELL/R decode regex char classes, and the decoder dimMaps. This touches the encoded wire format (ripple) and any persisted VCP strings; owner decision on the symbol.

title: MQTT packets use single-byte remaining-length; payloads >127 bytes are malformed (encode + decode)
detail: MQTT 'Remaining Length' is a variable-length quantity (1-4 bytes). The encoder writes it as one byte: `packet[1] = remainingLength;` in sendConnectPacket (line 234), publish (line 183), sendSubscribePacket (line 252), sendUnsubscribePacket (line 275). For any packet whose remaining length exceeds 127 this silently truncates/corrupts the length field. The receiver mirrors the bug: handlePublish reads `const remainingLength = bytes[offset++];` (line 303, comment admits 'simplified - assumes single byte') and then `payload = ...slice(offset, 2 + remainingLength)` (line 314), so multi-byte length tag payloads are mis-parsed. Real protocol bug; low real-world severity because the shipped static deploy is simulation-first and a live MQTT broker is unlikely to be reachable (no broker/CORS in the GitHub-Pages config).
fix: Implement proper MQTT variable-length-integer encoding/decoding (continuation-bit scheme) for both the writer (packet[1]...) and handlePublish's remaining-length read. Behavior-changing protocol work — owner decision.

title: Host applies untrusted peer MACHINE_LOCK/PLAYER_JOIN/PLAYER_LEAVE without authority validation → desync vector
detail: handleMessage trusts message.payload for every type with no validation. A malicious/buggy guest can send `{type:'MACHINE_LOCK', payload:{machineId, playerId}}` and the host applies `store.setMachineLock(message.payload.machineId, message.payload.playerId)` (lines 350-351) directly, bypassing the intent-handler authority check (machine-lock is otherwise routed through ADJUST intents in handleMachineIntent, useMultiplayerSync.ts:88-94). Similarly a guest can inject arbitrary PLAYER_JOIN/PLAYER_LEAVE (lines 288-303) to add/remove phantom players in everyone's store. Peers are untrusted (PeerJS WebRTC). This is primarily a state-consistency/desync correctness issue (and an authority bug); not a server-auth concern since there is no backend.
fix: On the host, gate MACHINE_LOCK/PLAYER_JOIN/PLAYER_LEAVE to host-authored broadcasts only: ignore inbound MACHINE_LOCK from guests (require it to flow via INTENT/handleMachineIntent), and ignore guest-originated PLAYER_JOIN/LEAVE. Validate payload.playerId matches the sender's connection identity. Behavior/contract change — owner decision.

title: handlePeerDisconnected can run twice for one peer (onClose + stale sweep) → double PLAYER_LEAVE broadcast
detail: A peer can be torn down via two independent paths: the PeerConnection onClose callback wired in handlePeerConnected (line 146 `onClose: () => this.handlePeerDisconnected(peerId)`) and the stale-connection sweep in the ping interval (lines 486-487). There is no guard that the peer is still present before acting, so for a peer that goes stale and then fires 'close', handlePeerDisconnected runs twice: it broadcasts PLAYER_LEAVE twice (lines 262-265) and calls store.removeRemotePlayer/removePeer twice. Store removals are idempotent, but the duplicate PLAYER_LEAVE broadcast is wasted/again-untrusted traffic.
fix: Early-return if the peer is already gone: `if (!this.peerConnections.has(peerId)) return;` at the top of handlePeerDisconnected.

title: calculateMachineVisuals omits machineName, so name-only machines resolve no SCADA prefix (visuals blank while store metrics sync)
detail: scadaToStoreMetrics passes machineName to getMachineTagPrefix (line 390, called as `scadaToStoreMetrics(machine.id, ..., machine.name)` in store.ts:179), enabling the display-name fallbacks for 'RM-101'/'SIFTER A'/'PACK LINE N' (getMachineTagPrefix lines 168-197). But calculateMachineVisuals calls `getMachineTagPrefix(machineId)` with no name (line 219, invoked from useSCADAVisuals.ts:30/47). For any machine whose id does NOT match the id-prefix patterns (silo-/rm-/sifter-/packer-) but whose name would, the prefix is null in calculateMachineVisuals → no temperature/vibration/rpm visuals, while scadaToStoreMetrics still syncs metrics. Inconsistent behavior between the two paths. Only bites if such name-only machines exist (roster ids not in types.ts/store.ts inline, unverified).
fix: Thread machineName through calculateMachineVisuals and pass it to getMachineTagPrefix, matching scadaToStoreMetrics. Confirm a name-only machine actually exists before treating as more than a consistency cleanup.

title: applyStateDiff treats the host's full STATE_SYNC as a diff; sequence number is ignored and emergencyActive is never applied on guests
detail: The host's STATE_SYNC payload (MultiplayerManager.ts:448-458) is a full snapshot (entire machines array + sequence + timestamp), not a diff, but is consumed by applyStateDiff as GameStateDiff. The `sequence` field is dropped (no ordering/staleness check — benign because the channel is reliable:true). More concretely, the emergencyActive branch is a no-op: `if (diff.emergencyActive && !gameStore.emergencyActive) { /* In a full implementation, this would trigger the full emergency sequence */ }` (lines 145-150) — the comment confirms guests never react to host emergency state, so a fire-drill/emergency started by the host does not propagate to guests.
fix: Implement the emergencyActive propagation on guests (call the gameSimulation emergency trigger) and rename/treat STATE_SYNC honestly as a snapshot. Behavior change — owner decision on the emergency-sync semantics.
